coredata.dc:title: Boron-doped graphene quantum dot/bismuth molybdate composite photocatalysts for efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reactions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhenyu
Luo, Min
Yuan, Shengbo
Meng, Linghu
Ding, Wenming
Su, Senda
Cao, Yue
Wang, Yingying
Li, Xiaoman
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth molybdate (BMO) is a promising visible-driven photocatalyst and constructing heterojunctions in BMO-based materials is an effective way to enhance photocatalytic performance. In this study, boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis and carbonization, followed by the preparation of bismuth molybdate/boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs/BMO) heterojunction photocatalysts using in-situ growth method. The introduction of BGQDs significantly improved the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity under the irradiation of visible light and without scavengers. The highest NH3 yield was achieved with BGQDs/BMO-10, which was 3.48 times higher than pure phase BMO. This improvement was due to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions between BGQDs and BMO with the synergistic mechanism of interfacial charge transport and the generation of more protons. This study provides useful guidance for enhancing the visible-light nitrogen fixation performance of BMO materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
In-situ growth method
BGQDs/BMO
Synergistic mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BMO and BGQDs/BMO nanocomposites
Band structure and charge transfer pathway
Evaluation of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of image BGQDs. The inset of (a) showed the particle size distribution histogram. (b) HRTEM image of BGQDs. TEM images of (c) BMO and (d, e) BGQDs/BMO-10. (f) HRTEM image of BGQDs/BMO-10.

(a) XRD patterns of BMO and BGQDs/BMO; (b) FT-IR spectra of BGQDs, BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10; XPS spectra of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 samples: (c) survey; (d) Bi 4f; (e) Mo 3d; (f) O 1s; and (g) C 1s; XPS spectra of BGQDs/BMO-10 sample: (h) B 1s.

(a) UV–vis DRS, (b) Tauc plots of BMO, BGQDs/BMO-5, BGQDs/BMO-10, BGQDs/BMO-20 and BGQDs/BMO-30; (c) UV–vis DRS, (d) Tauc plots of BGQDs; XPS valence band spectra of BMO (e), BGQDs (f) and BGQDs/BMO-10 (g).
(a) PL spectra (excitation wavelength of 365 nm) and (b) TRPL decay spectra of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10; (c) Transient photocurrent response plots of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10.

(a) Nitrogen fixation performance of ultrapure water without photocatalyst and without light irradiation in air atmosphere. (b) The NH4 + production rates of BMO, BGQDs/BMO-5, BGQDs/BMO-10, BGQDs/BMO-20, and BGQDs/BMO-30 under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation in N2; (c) NH4 + production rates in in dark condition and different atmospheres under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation; (d) The cyclic stability test of BGQDs/BMO-10. All experiments in Fig. 5a-d were repeated five times.
In-situ diffuse reflectance FT-IR spectra recorded during the photocatalytic NH3 synthesis over BGQDs/BMO-10.
Mechanism and interface charge transfer of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation by BGQDs/BMO-10.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of BGQDs and BGQDs/BMO-10 nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped graphene quantum dot/bismuth molybdate composite photocatalysts for efficient photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan, Ningxia 750021, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan, Ningxia 750021, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron-doped graphene quantum dots/Bi2MoO6 (BGQDs/BMO) heterojunction photocatalysts were prepared by in-situ growth method, which facilitated interfacial charge transfer and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The synergistic mechanism of interfacial charge transfer and generation of more protons greatly improved the nitrogen fixation efficiency of the composites.

Bismuth molybdate (BMO) is a promising visible-driven photocatalyst and constructing heterojunctions in BMO-based materials is an effective way to enhance photocatalytic performance. In this study, boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis and carbonization, followed by the preparation of bismuth molybdate/boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs/BMO) heterojunction photocatalysts using in-situ growth method. The introduction of BGQDs significantly improved the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity under the irradiation of visible light and without scavengers. The highest NH3 yield was achieved with BGQDs/BMO-10, which was 3.48 times higher than pure phase BMO. This improvement was due to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions between BGQDs and BMO with the synergistic mechanism of interfacial charge transport and the generation of more protons. This study provides useful guidance for enhancing the visible-light nitrogen fixation performance of BMO materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia is one of the largest inorganic chemical consumables in the world [1,2]. According to statistics, 80% of the world's annual synthetic ammonia is used in the production of nitrogen fertilizer to meet the booming development of agriculture, and the remaining 20% is used in industry, national defense, medicine and other fields [3–5]. Traditionally, ammonia has been produced mainly through enzymatic processes in plants and microorganisms [6]. However, it cannot meet the growing demand for ammonia. Therefore, efficient synthesis of ammonia is particularly important. At present, the most mature method of industrial ammonia synthesis is still the Haber-Bosch process. But it also has the disadvantage that it needs to operate at high temperatures and pressures and can cause environmental pollution.

In recent years, environmental pollution has gradually become a major obstacle limiting the progress of human social civilisation. Thus, there is an urgent demand for the development of an efficient and clean method of synthesising ammonia [7]. Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation is an effective technology for the artificial production of ammonia from the atmosphere. Due to its low-cost, high-performance, and environmentally friendly characteristics, this field is rapidly developing and receiving great attention from researchers [8,9]. In the field of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, the design of effective and viable semiconductor catalysts is a key element of success [10]. Bismuth-based materials are a new type of effective photocatalyst due to their low band gap energy and high separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The strong interaction between Bi 6p electrons and N 2p electrons in the bismuth-based material promotes the adsorption of N2 on its surface, allowing the NN bond to be weakened and the resulting activation of N2 to facilitate the eventual synthesis of NH3 [11,12]. BMO is the simplest substance in layered Aurivillius oxides. According to research reports, it has been confirmed that the conduction band of BMO is composed of Mo 4d and Bi 6p orbitals, while the valence band is mainly composed of O 2p orbitals [13]. This energy band structure is the reason for the narrow band gap of the BMO. It is considered to be a very promising candidate for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation because of its non-toxicity, easy availability of raw materials, high stability, and high responsiveness to visible light. However, the performance of pure BMO is not satisfactory due to the slow transmission and fast recombination of photogenerated carriers [14]. To solve the above problems, the photocatalysts can be modified by the following strategies to improve its photocatalytic activity. Common modification strategies include doping [15,16], compounding [17], and morphology control [18]. Hence, it is essential to select a suitable method for the modification of BMO for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation applications. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) a quasi-zero dimensional nanomaterial. Compared to semiconductor quantum dots containing heavy elements, it has low biological toxicity [19] and is an unparalleled environmentally friendly material. It is worth noting that GQDs can act as electron acceptors to promote electron transfer, thereby significantly improving light absorption and charge separation efficiency [20,21]. However, the low quantum yields of GQDs limit its development in photocatalysis [22]. According to research reports, it has been found that doping heteroatoms (N, B or B and N co-doped) in GQDs can enhance its quantum yield and improve the shortcoming of low quantum yield [23–25]. Inspired by the above content, the modification of BMO by BGQDs is beneficial to obtain high photocatalytic performance in the application of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. However, the mechanism by which BGQDs enhance the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of BMO needs to be further investigated.

This paper introduced a method for the preparation of BGQDs/BMO heterojunction photocatalysts by in-situ growth method and its application to photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. This approach enabled BGQDs to be successfully deposited on the surface of BMO microspheres. GQDs had excellent hydrophilicity due to its oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. However, common physical mixing methods with mild reaction conditions made it difficult to form chemical bonds, which could affect the performance of the final photocatalysts. Not suitable for the synthesis of BGQDs/BMO photocatalysts. Therefore, it was very suitable to prepare BGQDs/BMO photocatalysts by in-situ growth method. The systematic study of the microscopic morphology, crystal structure, optical properties, and energy band structure of photocatalysts could help us to analyse and propose the mechanism of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation. Compared with pure phase BMO, BGQDs/BMO-10 showed higher photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance, which improved by about 3.48 times. Through the energy band positions of BGQDs and BMO, BGQDs was found to play the role of junction component in the heterojunction structure constructed with BMO. It was further demonstrated that the introduction of BGQDs significantly increased the visible light absorption capacity and the rate of separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in BMO. In addition, the oxygen production of the photocatalysts was quantitatively tested, which further improved the mechanism of the photocatalytic nitrogen reduction.

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol (EG) and Na2MoO4·2H2O were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Boric acid (H3BO3) was purchased from Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd. (Yantai, China). Ethanol was obtained from Energy Chemical. All reagents mentioned above were of analytical grade and that no further purification was required before the experiments. The ultrapure water was provided throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were collected on a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) field emission transmission electron microscope. It had an accelerating voltage of 20–200 kV and was continuously adjustable. X-ray diffraction (XRD) datas were acquired at D8 ADVANCE A25 (Bruker, Germany) with rated voltage and rated current of 60 kV and 80 mA, respectively. Cu-Kα provided the radiation source for the tests. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and valence band spectra were performed on an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Scientific, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The work function (φ) was determined by calculating the difference between the photon energy and the binding energy of the secondary cutoff edge. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were tested on a Nicolet iN10 (Thermo Scientific, USA) Fourier transform micro-infrared spectrometer after grinding and pressing a mixture of the sample and KBr. The entire work involving absorbance and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were performed on a UV-3600i Plus (Shimadzu, Japan) UV–vis near infrared spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were carried out on an FLS1000 (Edinburgh, UK) at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The PLS-SXE 300 Xenon Light Source (Perfect Light Co., Ltd., Beijing) provided the light source for all photocatalytic tests throughout the work. Gas chromatography (GC) experiments were collected on a GC-2014C (Shimadzu, Japan). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were obtained on the EMXplus-6/1 (Bruker, Germany) paramagnetic resonance spectrometer.

BGQDs was synthesized by a one-step pyrolytic carbonation (Scheme 1 ). The specific steps were as follows: Boric acid (0.5 g) and citric acid (0.3 g) were added respectively in the crucible and stirred for the sufficient mixing. Then, the crucible containing the mixture was placed in a muffle furnace, heated at 200 °C and maintained for 2 h (here: the heating and cooling rates were both 3 °C/min). After being cooled to room temperature, a brownish-black solid powder was produced. Secondly, the solid powder was dissolved in ultrapure water. The aqueous solution was centrifuged several times at 8,000 rpm and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Finally, the obtained aqueous solution was dialyzed for 48 h and freeze-dried. A pale brownish-yellow solid powder, BGQDs, was finally obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BMO and BGQDs/BMO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: BGQDs/BMO nanocomposites were synthesized by the following steps using a one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1). To begin with, 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.5 mmol of Na2MoO4·2H2O were completely dissolved in 5 mL of EG, respectively. The mixed solution A was obtained by slowly dropping Na2MoO4 solution into Bi(NO3)3 solution under the condition of continuous stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution A was dropped into 20 mL of ethanol solution and stirred and mixed well, and the resulting mixture was defined as solution B. The pre-configured BGQDs solution (5, 10, 20, and 30 μL) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL were added to solution B, respectively. After stirring for 30 min, the final clarified solution obtained was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the obtained samples were washed three times with ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively, and dried at 70 °C overnight. Finally, they were defined as BGQDs/BMO-5, BGQDs/BMO-10, BGQDs/BMO-20, and BGQDs/BMO-30, respectively. In addition, the same procedure was followed to be able to synthesize BMO without the addition of BGQDs.

The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of all samples was evaluated under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) using a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source. The 30 mg of photocatalyst was fully dispersed in 100 mL of ultrapure water by sonication and then the dispersion was transferred to a quartz reactor with a circulating water jacket to maintain the temperature at around 25 °C. The dispersion containing the photocatalyst was stirred continuously in the dark for 30 mim before being tested for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. At the same time a high purity N2 was continuously injected at a flow rate of 100 mL/min to obtain a N2 saturated dispersion, enabling an adsorption–desorption equilibrium to be reached between the photocatalyst and the dissolved N2 and H2O. This was followed by irradiation with a 300 W xenon lamp and 5 mL of the dispersion was collected at regular intervals and then centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst. The NH4 +concentration could be determined by Nessler's reagent method. The UV–vis absorption spectra and standard curves of absorbance versus NH4 + concentration for the determination of NH4 + by Nessler's reagent were shown in Fig. S1.

The photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments, P. R. China) equipped with a standard three-electrode system. The working electrode was an fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass coated with photocatalyst (a slurry composed of a certain amount of photocatalyst, ethanol and Nafion solution was coated on the FTO glass), the counter electrode was a platinum sheet, the reference electrode was a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode and the electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. A 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm) was used as the light source for the measurement of linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and photocurrent response.

To investigate the micromorphology and particle size of the photocatalysts, BGQDs, BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 samples were characterised by TEM and HRTEM. Fig. 1 a showed the TEM image of the BGQDs, observing the good monodispersity of the BGQDs. The inset showed a histogram of the particle size distribution of the BGQDs, finding that the diameter of BGQDs was about 1.3 to 5 nm, with an average diameter of about 3.2 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) showed BGQDs had a high crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene [26]. Fig. 1c and d were TEM images of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10. BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 were observed to be microsphere structures consisting of small nanosheets. BGQDs/BMO-10 had no obvious morphological changes due to the introduction of BGQDs. Fig. 1e showed an enlarged TEM image of BGQDs/BMO-10. Numerous dark dots (BGQDs) were found to appear on the surface of the BMO after the addition of BGQDs, indicating that the BGQDs had successfully combined with the BMO. The HRTEM image of BGQDs/BMO-10 (Fig. 1f) clearly showed the lattice fringes of 0.21 and 0.315 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of BGQDs [27,28] and the (131) crystal plane of BMO [29,30], respectively. The above results strongly demonstrated the close contact between the BGQDs and BMO components of the composite.

To obtain the specific surface area of the photocatalysts, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm tests were carried out to calculate the specific surface area of the samples. As shown in Fig. S2, the specific surface area of the pure phase BMO was 71.78 m2 g−1. With the introduction of BGQDs, the specific surface area of BGQDs/BMO-10 increased to 75.14 m2 g−1. Overall, the specific surface area of BGQDs/BMO-10 had increased compared to pure phase BMO, but it was not significant. Therefore, the increase of specific surface area could improve the photocatalytic activity of BGQDs/BMO-10, but it was not the key factor affecting the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity.

Fig. 2 a showed the XRD patterns of pure phase BMO and BGQDs/BMO composites with different BGQDs contents. The diffraction peaks of BMO at 2 θ = 27.23°, 32.08°, 46.19°, and 55.12° corresponded to the (131), (002) (200), (260), and (331) crystal planes of the orthorhombic phase BMO (JCPDS Card No. 77–1246) respectively [31,32]. Fig. S3 showed the XRD pattern of BGQDs. A strong diffraction peak was observed at 2θ = 27.99° corresponding to the (002) crystalline plane of graphene, indicating that BGQDs had high crystallinity, which was consistent with the HRTEM results. No diffraction peaks of BGQDs were detected in the XRD patterns of the BGQDs/BMO composites, which indicated that the introduction of BGQDs had a negligible effect on the crystalline phase of BMO. This might be due to the low content of BGQDs in the composite and the high dispersion of BGQDs on the BMO surface [33]. In addition, no other impurity characteristic peaks were observed, indicating high purity of the prepared samples.

FT-IR spectroscopy could be used to study the structural composition of the sample or to determine its chemical groups. Fig. 2b showed the FT-IR spectra of the BMO, BGQDs, and BGQDs/BMO-10 composite. For pure phase BMO, the broad absorption band at 3424 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the OH bond. The absorption peaks at 841, 731, and 557 cm−1 corresponded to the bridging stretching vibration of the Mo-O-Mo bond, the stretching vibration of the Mo-O bond and the bending vibration of the Bi-O bond, respectively [34]. The five absorption peaks at 3216, 1719, 1617, 1451, and 1194 cm−1 in the spectra of BGQDs were associated with stretching vibrations of the OH bond, CO bond, CC bond, B-O [35,36] bond, and B-C bond [37]. The FT-IR spectrum of BGQDs/BMO-10 was similar to that of the pure phase BMO and the characteristic peaks of BGQDs were not clearly observed, probably due to the low content of BGQDs in this sample.

To further investigate the surface elements and chemical states of the materials, the BMO and 10 BGQDs/BMO composite were characterized by XPS. Before splitting the peaks, the signals of all elements detected were calibrated using the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. The XPS survey spectra of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 were shown in Fig. 2c. The chemical states of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 were examined by high-resolution XPS. As shown in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 159 and 164.4 eV were assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, indicating that Bi existed in the form of Bi3+ ions and that the Bi3+ ions were derived from [Bi2O2]2+ [38]. Fig. 2e showed the Mo 3d spectrum, in which the two peaks with binding energies of 232.3 and 235.4 eV were associated with Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of the Mo6+ ion [39]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) could be divided into three peaks, and the characteristic peaks at 529.9, 530.6, and 531.8 eV were labeled as lattice oxygen (Bi-O) (OL (Bi-O)), lattice oxygen (Mo-O) (OL (Mo-O)), and surface chemisorbed oxygen (Oads)/CO, respectively [40,41]. Meanwhile, the B 1s spectrum was also tested, but the signal-to-noise ratio of the B 1s spectrum was too low and exceeded the minimum detection limit of XPS. Therefore, the B 1s spectrum was not presented here. In the C 1s spectra (Fig. 2g), the peaks at 284, 284.5, 284.8, 285.8, and 288.5 eV for BGQDs/BMO-10 indicated the presence of C-B, CC, CC, CO, and O-CO bonds, respectively. However, the peaks of BMO at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.5 eV were attributed to CC, CO, and O-CO bonds, respectively, which resulted from the presence of contaminating carbon. Meanwhile, the B 1s spectrum was tested (Fig. 2h) and the peak at 193.7 eV was attributed to the B-C bond, demonstrating the presence of B in the BGQDs/BMO-10 sample. Fig. S4 could further explain the presence of B in BGQDs/BMO-10, while BMO did not contain B. The Bi 4f, Mo 3d and O 1s peaks of BGQDs/BMO-10 were shifted towards lower binding energies compared to the pure phase BMO, demonstrating that an interaction between BGQDs and BMO had occurred. The C 1s, O 1s, and B 1s spectra further verified that BGQDs were successfully introduced into BMO. The XPS atomic compositions of the BGQDs/BMO-10 sample were shown in Table S1, further reflecting the loading amounts of BGQDs on the BMO surface. It was found that the atomic % of Bi, Mo and O roughly followed its molecular formula. Furthermore, contaminating carbon led to an increase in the actual amount of carbon. The atomic % of B could also be obtained as 3.31 %. Fig. S5 showed the study of the chemical state of BGQDs. The XPS survey spectra of BGQDs was shown in Fig. S5 a. As shown in Fig. S5 b, the C 1s spectrum could be divided into five peaks, and the peaks at 284, 284.5, 284.8, 285.6, and 288.8 eV were attributed to C-B, CC, CC, CO, and O-CO bonds, respectively. The three peaks in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S5 c) centred at 531.7 eV, 532.4, and 533.2 eV indicated the presence of CO, B-O [24], and C-OH/COC bonds, respectively. Fig. S5 d showed the B 1s spectrum, and the observed peaks at 192.6 and 193.6 eV indicated the presence of boron in two forms of B-O and B-C bonds, respectively, fully demonstrating the successful synthesis of BGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band structure and charge transfer pathway
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optical absorption properties of the photocatalysts, UV–vis DRS tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption edges of BGQDs/BMO-5, BGQDs/BMO-10, BGQDs/BMO-20, and BGQDs/BMO-30 were gradually red-shifted from 502 nm to 508 nm compared to BMO. This could be attributed to the interaction between BGQDs and BMO, suggesting that the higher visible light responsiveness of the composites could enhance photocatalytic activity. The band gap values of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 could be roughly calculated based on Tauc plots (Fig. 3b), which were 2.62 and 2.60 eV, respectively. The reduction of the band gap promoted carrier transfer and thus improved photocatalytic performance. In addition, the absorption edge and band gap of BGQDs were 659 nm and 2.13 eV, respectively, as derived from Fig. 3c and d. The charge transfer pattern was further investigated by testing the XPS valence band spectra. The XPS valence band spectra in Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f showed valence band edge (EVB) values of 1.73, 2.81, and 1.63 eV (vs. NHE) for BMO, BGQDs and BGQDs/BMO-10, respectively. By using the equation ECB = EVB-Eg, the conduction band edge (ECB) values of −0.89, 0.68, and −0.97 eV were calculated for BMO, BGQDs and BGQDs/BMO-10 respectively.

To evaluate the charge separation performance of the photocatalysts, PL spectra were tested for BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10. In general, higher photoluminescence intensity means faster recombination rate of carrier pairs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PL intensity of BGQDs/BMO-10 was significantly reduced compared to that of the pure phase BMO, indicating that the introduction of BGQDs weakens the recombination ability of photogenerated electrons and holes [42]. Fig. 4b showed the time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10, observing that the fluorescence decay curves all fitted well into the triple exponential model. The datas for the three lifetimes (τ1-τ3), the relative amplitude (B) and the average lifetime (τavg.) obtained by fitting the fluorescence decay curves were shown in Table S2. The average lifetime of BMO was 13.57 ns, which was higher than the average lifetime of BGQDs/BMO-10 (7.76 ns), indicating that exciton dissociation was enhanced on BGQDs/BMO-10. The charge carriers generated by BGQDs/BMO-10 were more easily excited by lower energy light and rapidly trapped by the effective sites to prevent recombination, further demonstrating that BGQDs/BMO-10 might have higher photocatalytic performance compared to BMO [43,44]. To determine the bias voltage required for transient photocurrent response testing, LSV tests were carried out on BGQDs/BMO-10 under light and dark conditions. It was observed in Fig. S6 that the current values changed significantly after 0.5 V. Therefore, the bias voltage was determined to be 0.5 V. Fig. 4c showed the photocurrent density of the photocatalysts. The photocurrent density of BGQDs/BMO-10 composite was higher than that of BMO, which could be attributed to the heterogeneous structure of BGQDs and BMO. BGQDs/BMO-10 had the highest photocurrent density, which implied a high efficiency of photoinduced electron and hole separation [45,46], further explaining the reason for its optimal photocatalytic performance. Thus, all the characterization results confirmed that the introduction of BGQDs and the construction of BGQDs/BMO heterojunctions facilitated the interfacial charge transfer and the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the accuracy of the samples tested for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance, the ambient air quality was assessed. Fig. 5 a showed that in the air atmosphere, the presence of NH4 + was basically not detected in ultrapure water under the condition of without light irradiation and without photocatalyst. This indicated that the ambient air quality was good and suitable for photocatalytic nitrogen fixation testing. Fig. 5b showed the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation rates of BMO and BGQDs/BMO composites in ultrapure water under visible light irradiation. It was observed that the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation rates of the BGQDs/BMO composites were all higher than those of BMO, indicating that BGQDs could significantly improved the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation capacity of the BGQDs/BMO composites. The BGQDs/BMO-10 sample exhibited the highest photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. The NH4 +generation rate could reach 115.80 μmol·h−1·gcat -1 within 1 h, which was about 3.48 times higher than pure phase BMO. After 2 h of irradiation under visible light, its NH4 + generation rate could reach 157.04 μmol·h−1·gcat -1. However, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of the 20BGQDs/BMO and 30BGQDs/BMO samples showed a decreasing trend as the content of BGQDs increased. This was due to the fact that the aggregation of excessive BGQDs might block light absorption [47]. Fig. 5c showed that no NH4 + production were detected by the photocatalysts in control experiments under dark and Ar conditions. The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of BGQDs/BMO-10 was 3.69 and 3.48 times higher than that of BMO under air and N2 conditions respectively. Five cycle experiments showed that BGQDs/BMO-10 had good cycling stability (Fig. 5d). The sample after cyclic testing were characterized by TEM and XRD (Fig. S7 and S8). It was observed that the crystalline phase and morphology of the sample did not changed significantly, further confirming the high stability of the photocatalyst. Furthermore, the BGQDs/BMO-10 also showed excellent photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance compared to other photocatalysts (Table S3).

Tested by time-dependent in-situ diffuse reflectance FT-IR spectra. We had gained insight into the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction process of BGQDs/BMO-10. As shown in Fig. 6 , BGQDs/BMO-10 was exposed to humid N2 flow in the dark for 20 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. At the same time, the activation of N2 was also studied. Due to the activation of N2, an NH stretching vibration was observed at 3430 cm−1 [48]. The peaks at 1604 and 1417 cm−1 were attributed to the formation of surface NH4 + [49]. The peak at 1560 cm−1 belonged to the bending vibration of NH [50]. The peaks near 1329 and 1070 cm−1 belonged to the –NH2 swing [51] and the NN stretching vibration, respectively. Moreover, the intensity of the peak increased with the increase of irradiation time, which further indicated the smooth progress of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction.

To further investigate the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation mechanism of BGQDs/BMO-10, the oxygen vacancy (Ov) content of the photocatalysts were obtained through EPR detection. Fig. S9 showed that BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 exhibited symmetric EPR signals at a g-factor of approximately 2.003, indicating the presence of a certain amount of Ov, which was attributed to the electrons trapped on the Ov [52]. Furthermore, their signal intensities were essentially the same, further indicating that the introduction of BGQDs did not alter the Ov concentration of BMO. From this, it could be concluded that Ov was not the main reason for improving the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of BGQDs/BMO-10.

Based on the energy band structure of the photocatalysts, we could find that the band gaps of BMO (2.62 eV) and BGQDs/BMO-10 (2.60 eV) were very close and the EVB and ECB values of BGQDs/BMO-10 were lower than those of BMO. The upward shift of the conduction band (CB) position could enhance the reduction ability of electrons, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. Both BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10 could reduce nitrogen to ammonia because their CB potentials were more negative than the reduction potential of N2/NH3 (-0.092 eV vs. NHE) [53,54]. Furthermore, it was known from testing that the EVB and ECB values of the BGQDs were 2.81 and 0.68 eV, respectively (Fig. 7 ). Under visible light irradiation, BMO was excited to produce electrons (e-) and left holes (h+) in the valence band (VB). Due to the high electron mobility of GQDs, the e- on the CB of BMO couldn be transferred to the VB of BGQDs [55,56]. A Z-scheme heterojunction might be formed between the BGQDs and the BMO, allowing e- to flow out of the BMO and bind to h+ in the BGQDs. Finally, e- accumulated on the CB of BGQDs, which could react with N2 adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst and reduce to NH3. The h+ accumulated on the VB of BMO to form O2 via the H2O oxidation reaction. The difference in the band edge potentials of the photocatalysts was the main factor leading to the easy separation of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the photogenerated electron-holes moved rapidly in the opposite direction, resulting in slow charge recombination, which significantly enhanced the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity [57]. Furthermore, GC was also used to quantitatively test the photocatalytic oxygen production of BMO and BGQDs/BMO-10. As shown in Fig. S10, the oxygen production of BMO after 2 h of photocatalysis was 4.92 μmol·h−1·gcat -1, while the oxygen production of BGQDs/BMO-10 was 54.13 μmol·h−1·gcat -1. It could be seen that BGQDs/BMO-10 produced more O2. From equation (3), we knew that more O2 production meant more protons were produced. This indicated that more protons would participate in the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction to generate more ammonia. The reactions that might be involved in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation were as follows:
(1) BMO + hν → e- (CB, BMO) + h+ (VB, BMO)
(2) BGQDs + hν → e- (CB, BGQDs) + h+ (VB, BGQDs)
(3) 2H2O → O2 + 4H+ + 4e-
(4) N2 + 6H+ + 6e- → 2NH3

In this study, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of GQDs/BMO composites was explored and investigated for the first time without any sacrificial agents. It also provided a valuable reference for the synthesis of other doped GQDs/BMO composites. Moreover, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance had been greatly enhanced compared to that of the reported graphene-based materials/other bismuth-based materials [58,59]. In conclusion, BGQDs/BMO composites were successfully synthesized using the in-situ growth method, forming heterostructures with close contacts. BGQDs positively impacted the optical and electrochemical properties of BGQDs/BMO, and demonstrated impressive visible light photoactivity for N2 reduction to NH3. The best photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of 115.80 μmol·h−1·gcat -1 was achieved with BGQDs/BMO-10, which increased the NH3 yield by approximately 3.48 times compared to pure phase BMO. The introduction of BGQDs and the construction of BGQDs/BMO heterojunctions promoted interfacial charge transfer and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. Additionally, BGQDs/BMO-10 had a higher photocatalytic oxygen production capacity. It was revealed that BGQDs/BMO-10 was able to generate more protons and participate in the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation reaction. Based on the experimental results, it was clarified that the possible mechanism of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation was BGQDs enhanced the charge transfer rate of BMO materials and improved the generation rate of valence band holes, promoting the release of more e- from the CB and thus accelerating the rate of nitrogen reduction. This study proposes a new approach for constructing GQDs/BMO heterojunctions to enhance photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity and verifies its effectiveness, providing valuable insights for the development of this field.

Zhenyu Liu: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Min Luo: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shengbo Yuan: Software, Validation. Linghu Meng: Software, Validation. Wenming Ding: Data curation. Senda Su: Data curation. Yue Cao: Data curation. Yingying Wang: Data curation. Xiaoman Li: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

NXY-LXK2017A04).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.07.085.
